statement: In Q3 2028, CEO Will Coleman sold approximately 120,000 shares of Echoblast common stock via secondary sales to existing investors at an implied valuation of $680 million — $35 million below the Series B post-money.
Coleman's Q3 2028 secondary share sales were not disclosed to the full board until the Series C due-diligence process in early 2029.
The Audit Committee opened a preliminary inquiry into Coleman's Q3 2028 share sales in February 2029 to determine if they constituted a related-party transaction requiring board pre-approval.

Outside counsel Fenwick & West concluded in March 2029 that Coleman's share sales did not technically require board pre-approval under the existing stockholders' agreement but recommended retrospective ratification.
The board retrospectively ratified Coleman's secondary sales and adopted a new co-sale-rights policy requiring CEO pre-notification for any sales above 50,000 shares.
Coleman's share-sale incident was not disclosed in the S-1 filing or the IPO prospectus; the disclosure committee determined it was not material after the retrospective ratification.
In Q2 2033, an internal audit review resurfaced the 2028 Coleman share-sale matter after a shareholder-activist inquiry; the Audit Committee re-opened the file.
In September 2026, Echoblast onboarded a customer based in Shenzhen, China under a contract routed through a Singapore-incorporated intermediary, for GPU compute services.
The Shenzhen customer's workloads were flagged by an Echoblast engineer in November 2026 as potentially involving ITAR-controlled simulation data based on file-naming patterns observed in job logs.
General Counsel Miguel Black (then not yet hired; Angela Holmes handled the issue) escalated the Shenzhen-customer matter to outside counsel Cooley in December 2026.

Cooley's export-control review concluded in January 2027 that the workloads likely did not violate EAR/ITAR but recommended terminating the customer relationship as a precaution and filing a voluntary self-disclosure with BIS.
Echoblast terminated the Shenzhen customer contract in February 2027 and filed a voluntary self-disclosure with BIS, which was closed with no enforcement action in August 2027.
The Shenzhen-customer export-control incident was not publicly disclosed; the board determined it was not material and the BIS closure confirmed no violation.

In Q2 2028, Echoblast received a civil investigative demand (CID) from the FTC regarding marketplace pricing practices, specifically whether the marketplace take-rate structure constitutes anticompetitive tying.
Echoblast retained Morrison & Foerster to handle the FTC CID response.
Echoblast produced approximately 45,000 documents to the FTC over June-September 2028.
The FTC closed its investigation of Echoblast's marketplace practices in Q1 2029 without filing an enforcement action; the closure letter was received March 6, 2029.
The FTC inquiry was not publicly disclosed at any point; Cooley advised that no 8-K was required given the CID closure without action.
In August 2032, a power-distribution-unit failure at datacenter EBL-OR1 (Hillsboro) caused a 14-hour outage affecting approximately 6,200 customer GPU sessions.
The EBL-OR1 outage root cause was a faulty bypass switch in the facility's uninterruptible power supply (UPS), not a regional power-utility failure as publicly attributed.
Echoblast's public statement on the EBL-OR1 outage attributed the incident to a regional power-utility failure; internally, the ops team and legal team agreed this framing was technically misleading.
The EBL-OR1 outage triggered approximately $4.2 million in SLA credits to affected enterprise customers.
The EBL-OR1 QTS colocation partner's insurance dispute over the UPS failure is ongoing as of Q4 2033.
Head of fleet reliability Jacob Evans submitted his resignation in the week following the EBL-OR1 outage; CEO Coleman personally convinced him to stay by offering a retention package.

A former CoreWeave senior engineer hired by Echoblast in March 2026 (Roman Munoz, Head of BD / datacenter leases) brought with him knowledge of CoreWeave's datacenter site-selection methodology and vendor-pricing benchmarks.
CoreWeave's outside counsel sent a cease-and-desist letter to Echoblast in July 2026 alleging that Munoz had retained confidential CoreWeave documents on a personal device.
Echoblast conducted an internal forensic review of Munoz's devices and found approximately 340 CoreWeave-sourced documents including pricing models and vendor quotes.
Echoblast settled with CoreWeave confidentially in October 2026 for an undisclosed amount; Munoz was placed on administrative leave for two weeks and returned with a formal written warning.
The CoreWeave settlement required Echoblast to certify deletion of all retained documents and implement a clean-room policy for competitive-hire onboarding.

In January 2030, Cursor's VP of Engineering emailed Echoblast's CRO Robert Graves indicating Cursor was evaluating a full migration of its Echoblast training workloads to CoreWeave due to pricing concerns.
Echoblast's internal response to the Cursor migration threat included a 22% discount on Cursor's reserved-capacity contract, approved by CEO Coleman over CFO Tran's objection.
The Cursor migration threat was not disclosed to investors during the IPO roadshow in February-March 2030 despite Cursor being named in the S-1 as a significant customer.
Cursor ultimately stayed with Echoblast after the discount and expanded its contract in Q3 2030.
Pika's churn from Echoblast in late 2031 was partly driven by Pika discovering it was being charged 8% more than a comparable-scale customer for equivalent GPU configurations.

In H1 2031, CFO Luis Tran submitted approximately $340,000 in personal expense reimbursements through the corporate expense system that were later flagged by internal audit as potentially non-business-related.
The flagged expenses included approximately $180,000 in travel charges to destinations with no documented business purpose and $95,000 in vendor payments to a company controlled by Tran's spouse.
The Audit Committee engaged EY Forensic & Integrity Services in October 2031 to conduct an independent review of CFO Tran's expense submissions.

EY's review concluded in January 2032 that approximately $275,000 of the $340,000 flagged did not meet the company's T&E policy requirements; however, intent to defraud could not be conclusively established.
CFO Tran repaid $275,000 to Echoblast in February 2032 and received a formal written warning from the board; his FY2031 bonus was reduced to zero.
The board considered terminating Tran but concluded that the disruption risk (pre-annual 10-K filing cycle, CFO replacement timeline) outweighed the severity of the confirmed policy violations.
The CFO expense matter was not disclosed in the FY2031 10-K or the FY2031 proxy statement; the disclosure committee determined it was not individually material.
In Q3 2033, a second internal-audit review identified an additional $85,000 in CFO Tran expense submissions from H1 2033 with similar patterns to the 2031 incidents.
The board initiated a confidential CFO-succession planning process in September 2033, in parallel with the second expense review.

Echoblast's Series B pre-close valuation originally cleared at $800 million; the final post-money was revised down to $715 million after a last-minute due-diligence finding related to marketplace partner contracts.

The Series B valuation adjustment was driven by General Catalyst's discovery that approximately $8 million of Echoblast's $55M ARR at the time came from a single marketplace partner whose contract had a 90-day termination clause.
Echoblast renegotiated the at-risk partner's contract to a 12-month minimum before the Series B closed; the partner (a European colocation provider) was retained.
The Series B repricing was communicated to the seed and Series A investors (Conviction, Greylock) but not publicly disclosed.
Echoblast's NVIDIA preferred-supply side-letter (2027-2028) included a clause prohibiting Echoblast from offering AMD MI-series GPUs to customers doing more than $500K/month in inference workloads.
The NVIDIA-Echoblast side-letter's anti-AMD clause was reviewed by outside counsel and flagged as potentially anticompetitive if disclosed publicly.
Echoblast allowed the anti-AMD clause to expire at end-2028 and began offering AMD MI300X and MI325X GPUs from Q2 2029 onward.
NVIDIA NVentures' Series D investment included a non-binding letter-of-intent for Echoblast to maintain at least 85% NVIDIA GPU share in its fleet through 2031.
Echoblast's actual NVIDIA GPU fleet share declined to approximately 79% by end-2031 as AMD demand grew, technically breaching the letter-of-intent; NVIDIA did not formally object.
In Q3 2032, a hyperscaler (internally code-named "Atlas") approached Echoblast through Goldman Sachs about a potential strategic acquisition at an implied valuation of approximately $55 billion.
The board authorized CEO Coleman and GC Miguel Black to engage in preliminary due-diligence discussions with Atlas under a mutual NDA.
Atlas provided Echoblast with a preliminary non-binding indication of interest at $130-$145 per share (representing a 35-50% premium over the trailing 30-day VWAP at the time).
The board voted 5-2 to decline Atlas's indication of interest in October 2032, with independent directors Hawkins and Ward voting to continue discussions.
Atlas made a revised approach in Q2 2033 at $105-$115 per share (lower due to macro-recession-driven multiple compression); the board declined without extended discussion.
The Atlas acquisition discussions were never publicly disclosed; the board determined disclosure was not required as no definitive agreement was reached.
Will Coleman's brother, James Coleman, held a 0.3% equity stake in an Echoblast marketplace partner (a small Texas-based colocation provider) at the time of Echoblast's seed round.
The Texas colocation provider received approximately $420,000 in marketplace partner payouts from Echoblast in 2026, while James Coleman remained an equity holder.
Angela Holmes identified the related-party issue during pre-Series-A cleanup in September 2026 and required James Coleman to divest his colocation stake before the Series A closed.

James Coleman completed his divestiture of the Texas colocation stake in October 2026; the related-party disclosure in the S-1 footnotes references this historical arrangement without naming the individual.
The EBL-NV1 datacenter (Las Vegas) experienced a cooling-system leak in October 2029 that damaged approximately 180 GPUs, resulting in $1.8 million in replacement costs.
The NV1 cooling leak was caused by a contractor installation error during initial operations; the contractor's insurance covered approximately 60% of the replacement cost.
Echoblast's board discussed but ultimately rejected pursuing a dual-class share structure at IPO; Coleman initially favored 10:1 supervoting but independent directors Hawkins and Ward objected.
The IPO lockup included a non-standard exception allowing NVIDIA NVentures to sell up to 15% of its holdings during the lockup period for portfolio-rebalancing purposes.
Mark Howell (CTO) considered leaving Echoblast in mid-2028 to join a stealth foundation-model lab; Coleman and Holmes negotiated a $12 million retention grant (4-year vest) to retain him.
Howell's retention grant was approved by the Compensation Committee without disclosure to the full Series B investor syndicate.
Howell's retention grant was disclosed in the S-1 as a one-time key-employee retention award but the circumstances (near-departure) were not described.

In Q4 2032, Echoblast's internal security team discovered that a marketplace partner had been running cryptocurrency-mining workloads on Echoblast-branded capacity, violating the marketplace terms of service.
The crypto-mining marketplace partner had been operating for approximately 4 months before detection, billing approximately $1.2 million in customer charges during that period.
Echoblast terminated the offending marketplace partner and issued refunds totaling $890,000 to affected customers; the incident was not publicly disclosed.
The marketplace-fraud incident prompted Echoblast to implement mandatory workload-attestation for all marketplace partners starting Q1 2033.
Echoblast's Q1 2033 earnings missed sell-side consensus by approximately 6% on revenue, the first quarterly miss since IPO.
Internal pre-call analysis attributed the Q1 2033 miss primarily to two enterprise customers delaying contract renewals into Q2, but IR messaging emphasized macro headwinds.
Shopify's Q2 2033 Executive Business Review revealed Shopify was reducing its Echoblast committed spend by approximately 30% for FY2034 due to an internal build-vs-buy decision.
The Shopify spend reduction was not communicated to investors in Q2 2033 guidance commentary; management described the revenue outlook as 'broadly stable across the customer base.'
Echoblast's Atlas acquisition-talk NDA prohibits either party from disclosing the discussions for 18 months after termination; the NDA expires in April 2034.
Echoblast's IPO pricing process included a last-minute $2/share reduction from the initial filing range of $34-$37 after feedback from the Morgan Stanley syndicate desk.
Coleman proposed pricing at $35 despite the syndicate feedback; the board overruled 4-3 in favor of $32 to ensure a healthy first-day pop.
In Q4 2033, the Echoblast AI Safety team flagged that a customer was using Echoblast Inference endpoints to serve an unrestricted chatbot to minors, violating Echoblast's acceptable-use policy.
The flagged customer refused to add content-safety filters; Echoblast's internal escalation took 11 days before the customer was suspended, during which the service remained live.
The AUP-violation customer was generating approximately $95,000/month in Echoblast revenue, contributing to the delay in enforcement escalation according to the post-incident review.
The safety team's post-incident report recommended mandatory 48-hour enforcement SLAs for AUP violations; as of Q4 2033, the recommendation has been approved but not yet implemented.

Echoblast's FY2032 annual engineering compensation review revealed that three senior engineers (L7+) had total compensation packages exceeding CEO Coleman's base salary, prompting a board-level discussion about executive-compensation competitiveness.
Mark Leonard (Chief Architect) has a standing arrangement allowing him to work 80% time (4 days/week) since 2031 — a fact known internally but not disclosed in SEC filings or public leadership pages.

Echoblast's internal model-serving benchmarks in Q2 2033 showed that AMD MI350X GPUs outperformed NVIDIA H200 on specific Llama-family inference workloads by approximately 18% on cost-per-token, creating internal pressure to shift fleet mix.
The AMD-outperformance finding was shared only with the CTO and Chief Architect; it was not included in the Q2 2033 investor-day presentation materials.
Echoblast's Audit Committee discovered in November 2033 that CFO Tran had authorized a $2.4 million payment to a consulting firm owned by a former Echoblast employee without competitive bidding.
The consulting-firm payment accelerated the board's CFO-succession timeline; as of December 2033, the board expects to announce Tran's departure in Q1 2034.
Echoblast's internal whistleblower hotline received 14 reports in FY2033; 3 were substantiated and resulted in corrective action.
One of the three substantiated FY2033 whistleblower reports involved a marketplace BD manager accepting a $15,000 gift from a prospective marketplace partner; the manager was terminated.
Echoblast's EBL-VA2 (Ashburn) colocation partner (Equinix) notified Echoblast in August 2033 that it would not extend the current lease term beyond 2035 due to facility-redevelopment plans.
Echoblast's internal planning team identified a replacement site in Manassas, Virginia; lease negotiations are in progress as of Q4 2033.
Echoblast's internal model shows that losing the EBL-VA2 site without replacement would reduce US-East capacity by approximately 22% and affect 4 Tier-1 customer deployments.

Angela Holmes's ex-husband holds approximately 45,000 Echoblast shares via a community-property distribution from their 2030 divorce; this holding is disclosed in SEC filings but the divorce circumstances are not.
Will Coleman's total compensation in FY2033 was approximately $24.5 million (salary $650K, bonus $975K, equity grants $22.4M, other $475K), making him one of the higher-paid neocloud CEOs.
In FY2033, institutional proxy advisory firm ISS recommended a vote against Echoblast's say-on-pay proposal citing above-median CEO compensation relative to TSR performance.
Echoblast's FY2033 say-on-pay vote passed with 62% support — the lowest in the company's post-IPO history and below the typical 80%+ threshold for cloud-infrastructure peers.
Echoblast's board discussed pivoting to a quarterly dividend policy in Q3 2033 to attract income-oriented institutional investors; the proposal was tabled pending the macro-recession recovery.
Echoblast's internal projections estimate that the FY2034 GPU capex requirement of $1.6B will require either a secondary offering or a draw on the JPMorgan revolving facility.
Echoblast has been in preliminary discussions with NVIDIA about a potential joint-venture for a dedicated AI-training supercomputer site in Texas, code-named "Project Firestorm".
Project Firestorm's proposed structure would have NVIDIA contribute GPUs at-cost and Echoblast contribute the facility and operations, with revenue shared 60/40 (Echoblast/NVIDIA).

Echoblast's internal legal team identified potential antitrust concerns with the Project Firestorm revenue-sharing structure given NVIDIA's market dominance in GPU supply; discussions paused in October 2033.

Echoblast's Q4 2033 board strategy offsite identified three strategic options for FY2034-2035: (A) organic growth at 22% YoY, (B) an aggressive M&A play acquiring 2-3 smaller GPU-cloud providers, or (C) exploring a take-private via PE.
The take-private option (C) was championed by Blackstone Growth (Series D co-lead) and received preliminary interest from two large PE firms; the board agreed to continue evaluating in Q1 2034.
Echoblast's post-IPO lockup expiration in September 2030 saw approximately 18 million shares sold by insiders and early investors in the first week, causing a 9% stock-price decline.
Conviction Partners (seed lead Akira Wright) sold its entire remaining Echoblast position during the September 2030 lockup-expiration window.
Echoblast's EBL-EU1 Frankfurt site experienced a data-sovereignty compliance flag in Q2 2032 when EU customer workloads were briefly routed through EBL-OR1 (US) during a failover event.

The EU data-sovereignty failover incident affected approximately 12 customer accounts and lasted 47 minutes; Echoblast self-reported to the relevant EU data-protection authority, which closed the matter without penalty.
Echoblast's engineering team built a cross-region-failover kill-switch ("Project Shield") in response to the EU data-sovereignty incident; it became GA in Q3 2032.

Echoblast's co-founder Mark Leonard holds a side advisory role at a Princeton-based robotics startup, Kinesis Labs, for which he receives $50,000/year in advisory fees; this is disclosed internally but not in SEC filings.

Kinesis Labs uses Echoblast Cloud for its training workloads under a standard self-service account; the advisory relationship is flagged in Echoblast's related-party log but not in the proxy's related-party section.

Echoblast's Series C term sheet included a drag-along provision giving Fidelity, T. Rowe, and Altimeter the right to force a sale if holders of 65%+ of preferred stock approve; this provision survived the IPO as a dormant charter clause.
Echoblast's FY2033 10-K is expected to include a going-concern-adjacent risk factor for the first time, citing the EBL-VA2 lease non-renewal and FY2034 capex funding gap.

Echoblast's internal cyber-incident-response plan was activated twice in FY2033: once for a phishing campaign targeting engineering credentials (contained in 4 hours, no data loss) and once for the AUP-violation customer incident.
Echoblast is evaluating a potential secondary listing on the London Stock Exchange to improve EU investor access and liquidity; preliminary discussions with advisors began in November 2033.
Echoblast's Q4 2033 internal customer-satisfaction survey showed an NPS of 41 for enterprise accounts and 28 for self-service accounts; the self-service NPS was the lowest in four quarters.

Echoblast's internal culture survey in H2 2033 revealed that 23% of respondents rated CEO Coleman's leadership as 'needs improvement' — a new high, attributed internally to the RIF and macro-uncertainty.

Echoblast's board privately discussed whether CEO Coleman should transition to Executive Chairman with an external operator-CEO hired for the next growth phase; this discussion remains unresolved as of Q4 2033.
